followups.q: How do deletion vectors interact with SCD Type 2 tables in Lakeflow?
followups.a: Deletion vectors are opt-in per table via ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true'). On an APPLY CHANGES SCD Type 2 target the engine uses them when back-filling __END_AT on closed rows: rather than rewriting the Parquet file, a deletion vector marks the old row logically deleted and the new row is appended. This reduces write amplification substantially on large SCD2 tables with frequent updates. Reads are slightly heavier because they must merge the deletion vector, but Photon handles this efficiently.

q: Great Expectations on Databricks: a senior data engineer asks whether to store Expectation Suites in the GE local filesystem context, DBFS, or Unity Catalog Volumes. What are the tradeoffs?
a: For production Great Expectations on Databricks there are three realistic storage options for Expectation Suites and validation results. Local filesystem (driver node) is ephemeral — suites disappear when the cluster terminates, so it is only valid for interactive exploration. DBFS was the traditional choice: suites stored under /dbfs/great_expectations/ persist across cluster restarts and are accessible from all nodes. However DBFS is a legacy path — Databricks is moving to Unity Catalog Volumes. Unity Catalog Volumes (/Volumes/catalog/schema/vol/great_expectations/) integrate with UC governance: access is controlled by UC grants, lineage is tracked, and you get cross-workspace replication via Delta Sharing. A DataContext backed by a UC Volume also pairs cleanly with the streaming ingestion pattern because the same service principal that runs Auto Loader jobs can read/write the GE context. For new projects in 2025+ always use UC Volumes. DBFS is acceptable for brownfield migrations. The GE DataContext configuration (great_expectations.yml) must point datasources at the UC path and validation_result_stores / checkpoint_store must also target the UC Volume path.
fj: I would store Expectation Suites on DBFS because that's where I store everything in Databricks.
fs: Use Unity Catalog Volumes for Great Expectations Suites on Databricks (2025+): UC-native governance, lineage, and grants; DBFS is legacy. Local filesystem is ephemeral and not suitable for production.

followups.q: How do you configure a Great Expectations DataContext to read from a Unity Catalog Volume?
followups.a: Set the GE store backend root_directory to the UC Volume FUSE path: /Volumes/catalog/schema/vol/great_expectations/. In great_expectations.yml configure stores with base_directory pointing to that path. FUSE access to UC Volumes is available on Databricks Runtime 13.3+ on single-user or shared clusters with UC enabled. Alternatively use the GE Databricks datasource with a Spark DataFrame source backed by spark.read.table() referencing a UC table.

q: Compare streaming ingestion latency profiles for Auto Loader (file notification) vs Kafka with Structured Streaming on Databricks. When does each dominate and what ops overhead differs?
a: Auto Loader file notification mode achieves end-to-end streaming ingestion latency of roughly 10–60 seconds from file landing in cloud storage to row visibility in bronze Delta, because it depends on cloud storage event notifications (SQS/SNS for S3, Event Grid for ADLS) and then a Structured Streaming micro-batch trigger. Kafka with Structured Streaming can achieve sub-second to ~5 second latency because Kafka consumers poll continuously and micro-batch durations can be as short as 100 ms. Use Auto Loader when your data source is file-based (object storage drops from upstream systems, ETL exports, CDC snapshots), you can tolerate 10–60s latency, and you want zero-ops streaming ingestion with no broker to manage. Use Kafka when latency SLAs are under 10 seconds, your upstream system natively produces to Kafka, or you need replayable topic retention for multiple consumers. Ops overhead: Auto Loader requires cloud IAM for storage + event notification permissions, a Databricks secrets scope for credentials, and periodic schema inference. Kafka Structured Streaming requires broker connectivity, consumer group management, offset checkpoint management, and separate monitoring of consumer lag. Neither is universally simpler — the right choice follows the upstream system's native interface.
fj: Auto Loader is always simpler so I would use it for all streaming ingestion.
fs: Auto Loader (file notification) delivers 10–60s end-to-end streaming ingestion latency and is zero-ops for file-native sources; Kafka Structured Streaming achieves sub-second latency but adds broker ops. Choose based on upstream system and latency SLA.

followups.q: Can you use Auto Loader and Kafka Structured Streaming in the same Lakeflow pipeline?
followups.a: Yes. A single Lakeflow pipeline can declare multiple streaming tables sourced from different streaming ingestion origins — one @dlt.table reading from cloudFiles (Auto Loader), another from kafka. They run as separate Structured Streaming queries within the same pipeline process. The pipeline manages checkpoints for each stream independently.

q: Your streaming ingestion pipeline uses Auto Loader in file notification mode and you notice it has started missing files after a storage bucket policy change. How do you diagnose and recover?
a: Auto Loader file notification mode relies on a cloud event notification pipeline (SQS queue + SNS topic for S3; Event Grid + Azure Queue for ADLS). If the storage bucket policy change revoked the permission for Auto Loader's IAM role to push or read from the notification queue, new file events are dropped silently — Auto Loader will not throw an error, it simply sees no new files. Diagnosis steps: 1) Check the Auto Loader source metrics in the Structured Streaming UI — if inputRowsPerSecond is zero while files are being uploaded, the notification pipeline is broken. 2) Inspect the cloud-side event notification queue (SQS/Azure Queue) for message count — if count is growing, the consumer (Auto Loader) is not polling; if count is zero, events are not being published. 3) Review IAM/RBAC: the Databricks instance profile or service principal must have sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage, s3:GetObject on the bucket. Recovery: fix the IAM policy, then restart the stream — Auto Loader will fall back to directory listing mode for a catch-up scan if configured with cloudFiles.useNotifications=false temporarily, or you can switch to directory listing mode permanently and accept higher listing overhead. Alternatively provision a new SQS/SNS stack and update the Auto Loader option cloudFiles.queueUrl. Streaming ingestion continuity depends entirely on this IAM chain being intact.
fj: I would restart the cluster because Auto Loader sometimes gets stuck.
fs: Auto Loader file notification failures are almost always an IAM/permissions break on the cloud event notification queue — diagnose by checking stream metrics and SQS/Azure Queue depth, then fix the IAM policy. Restarting the cluster does not fix a permission problem.

followups.q: How does deletion vectors configuration affect Auto Loader's write path to bronze Delta?
followups.a: Deletion vectors are opt-in and apply to UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE operations on the Delta table. Auto Loader performs only INSERT operations (append-only to bronze), so deletion vectors have no effect on the Auto Loader write path. They become relevant only if you later run MERGE or DELETE on the bronze table for deduplication or compaction.

q: A data quality team wants to use Great Expectations alongside Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines expectations — not replace them. Design the two-layer quality architecture for a streaming ingestion medallion pipeline.
a: A two-layer quality architecture uses Lakeflow Expectations (native) as the fast, structural first line of defense, and Great Expectations as the deeper statistical second layer run asynchronously. Layer 1 — Lakeflow Expectations inside the pipeline: enforce structural invariants using @dlt.expect_or_drop (null checks, enum values, range constraints) directly on streaming tables in the bronze→silver transformations. These are SQL predicates evaluated per row at pipeline execution time with zero latency overhead. Violations surface in the pipeline event log and Unity Catalog data quality metrics. Layer 2 — Great Expectations in a downstream audit job: after silver is written, a scheduled Databricks job runs a GE Checkpoint on the silver table using Delta CDF to pick up only new rows. GE Expectations here cover distribution checks (expect_column_mean_to_be_between, expect_column_kl_divergence_to_be_less_than), cross-column consistency (expect_column_pair_values_a_to_be_greater_than_b), and volume anomalies (expect_table_row_count_to_be_between). GE publishes Data Docs to a UC Volume path so stakeholders have a human-readable quality report. Integration: the GE audit job writes a quality_summary Delta table; a Databricks SQL alert monitors it. When Lakeflow Expectations drop rows, those rows appear in the quarantine table — the GE job can also run on the quarantine table to characterize failure patterns. Streaming ingestion health is thus validated at two fidelity levels without coupling quality to ingestion latency.
fj: I would just use Lakeflow Expectations because they're already built in.
fs: Two-layer quality: Lakeflow Expectations for per-row structural invariants inside the pipeline (zero latency, native), Great Expectations in an async downstream job for distributional and cross-column assertions (richer, human-readable Data Docs). Neither replaces the other.

followups.q: How do you surface Great Expectations Data Docs inside Databricks for stakeholders?
followups.a: Store Data Docs at a UC Volume path or an S3/ADLS bucket with public read or pre-signed URL access. After each GE Checkpoint run, call context.build_data_docs() to regenerate the HTML report. Embed the Data Docs index URL in a Databricks SQL Dashboard using a text tile with an iframe, or post the URL to Slack via a GE Slack action on the checkpoint.

q: Structured Streaming on Databricks writes to a Delta table that is also the source for a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline. What ordering and isolation guarantees exist, and where can race conditions occur?
a: This pattern — a Structured Streaming job writing to bronze Delta, which a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline then reads via dlt.read_stream() — is a common streaming ingestion architecture but has subtle isolation concerns. Delta Lake provides snapshot isolation: each Structured Streaming micro-batch that writes to bronze commits a transaction, and the Lakeflow DLT pipeline's streaming read sees committed snapshots in order. There is no dirty read — partial micro-batches are never visible. Race conditions: 1) If the Structured Streaming job and the Lakeflow pipeline share the same Delta table as both reader and writer, concurrent reads during an active OPTIMIZE on the table can cause the stream to see a new file list mid-scan — OPTIMIZE on Delta is safe because it commits atomically. 2) Schema changes in the bronze Delta table (e.g., Auto Loader adds a new column via schema evolution) happen between commits. The Lakeflow pipeline may encounter schema mismatch until it refreshes its schema — enable dlt schema evolution with spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled', 'true') at the pipeline level. 3) Deletion vectors opt-in: if deletion vectors are enabled on the bronze table and a compaction job runs while the DLT pipeline reads, the read handles deletion vector merging transparently — no race. The critical guarantee: streaming ingestion from Auto Loader → bronze Delta → Lakeflow read_stream is safe as long as each layer only appends; concurrent MERGE or DELETE on bronze from a third writer would be a genuine race.
fj: Delta Lake handles concurrency so there are no race conditions to worry about.
fs: Delta's snapshot isolation prevents dirty reads between Structured Streaming writes and Lakeflow reads, but schema evolution gaps (Auto Loader new columns) and third-party MERGE/DELETE on bronze are real race risks. Enable schema auto-merge and keep bronze append-only.

followups.q: What happens to the Lakeflow pipeline if the upstream Structured Streaming job falls behind and bronze gets a large backlog?
followups.a: The Lakeflow pipeline will process the backlog in chunks bounded by its trigger cadence and maxFilesPerTrigger / maxBytesPerTrigger options. The pipeline event log shows increasing pending files. In continuous mode it catches up automatically. In triggered mode each run drains a bounded set of files. Streaming ingestion SLA degradation flows downstream: if silver/gold have refresh SLAs, alert on the Lakeflow pipeline's last_updated timestamp rather than the stream lag directly.

q: How does Great Expectations' 'spark' datasource differ from its 'pandas' datasource on Databricks, and when does each matter for streaming ingestion validation?
a: Great Expectations has two primary DataFrame-backed datasources on Databricks: the Spark datasource (SparkDFDataset or the new Fluent API Spark datasource) evaluates Expectations as Spark DataFrame operations distributed across the cluster, while the Pandas datasource (PandasDataset) converts the DataFrame to Pandas on the driver and runs NumPy/Pandas operations locally. For streaming ingestion validation the distinction is critical. Spark datasource: correct for large micro-batches or batch validation of bronze/silver tables with millions of rows. Expectations translate to DataFrame filter/count operations — expect_column_values_to_not_be_null becomes df.filter(col.isNull()).count(). Distributed, no OOM risk. Pandas datasource: only safe for small DataFrames (< a few million rows). If you call df.toPandas() inside a foreachBatch on a high-throughput streaming ingestion pipeline, you risk driver OOM and add serialization overhead that stalls the stream. Best practice: use the Spark datasource for Great Expectations validation on all Databricks streaming ingestion pipelines. Reserve Pandas for local development and unit tests against sample data. In the Fluent API: context.sources.add_spark('spark_src') returns a Spark-backed asset; context.sources.add_pandas_filesystem() returns a Pandas-backed asset.
fj: I would convert the DataFrame to Pandas before running Great Expectations since GE is a Python library.
fs: Use the Spark datasource for Great Expectations on Databricks streaming ingestion — it runs Expectations as distributed Spark operations. The Pandas datasource serializes to driver and risks OOM on large batches; reserve it for local dev and small sample tests.

followups.q: Can Great Expectations run an Expectation that spans two columns without converting to Pandas?
followups.a: Yes — expect_column_pair_values_a_to_be_greater_than_b and expect_multicolumn_sum_to_equal are implemented as Spark DataFrame filter/count operations in the Spark datasource. They run distributed. Custom expectations using @column_pair_map_expectation or @multicolumn_map_expectation decorators also render as Spark UDFs when using the Spark datasource.

q: Walk through a production streaming ingestion architecture on Databricks where deletion vectors are enabled on bronze Delta and explain how that changes compaction and query behavior.
a: In a production streaming ingestion architecture, Auto Loader writes JSON events to a bronze Delta table via Structured Streaming in a Lakeflow pipeline. Without deletion vectors (the default before they became opt-in), any DELETE or MERGE on bronze — such as a deduplication job removing duplicate event IDs — rewrites entire Parquet data files even if only 1% of rows were deleted. With deletion vectors enabled (ALTER TABLE bronze_events SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true')), a DELETE operation instead writes a small deletion vector file that marks deleted row IDs; the Parquet data files are untouched. This dramatically reduces write amplification for the dedup job — a critical concern when bronze tables are large and dedup runs frequently. Read-side behavior: when a reader scans bronze, the Delta reader merges the deletion vector to skip marked rows. Photon handles this natively with minimal overhead. Compaction (OPTIMIZE): when OPTIMIZE runs, it physically rewrites Parquet files and incorporates all pending deletion vectors — the output files are clean with no deletion vectors. After OPTIMIZE the deletion vector files for the compacted file set are removed. The streaming ingestion path (Auto Loader appends) is unaffected by deletion vectors — appends never generate deletion vectors. Deletion vectors are opt-in per table; enabling them on bronze is safe and recommended when your bronze table receives any dedup or CDC MERGE operations alongside the streaming ingestion writes.
fj: Deletion vectors just make reads slower so I would not enable them on a high-throughput bronze table.
fs: Deletion vectors are opt-in and reduce write amplification for DELETE/MERGE on bronze by writing small marker files instead of rewriting Parquet; reads are minimally slower (Photon-accelerated). The streaming ingestion append path is entirely unaffected. Enable them on bronze tables that receive dedup or CDC merges.

followups.q: How do you verify deletion vectors are being used on a Delta table after enabling them?
followups.a: Run DESCRIBE DETAIL table_name and check the properties field for delta.enableDeletionVectors = true. After running a DELETE, run DESCRIBE HISTORY table_name — the operation entry will show numDeletionVectorsAdded > 0. You can also inspect the _delta_log JSON entry for the DELETE commit and look for the deletionVector field in the remove/add actions.

q: A Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline using APPLY CHANGES INTO for SCD Type 2 is producing unexpected duplicate current rows. How do you diagnose and fix it, considering streaming ingestion source deduplication and the SEQUENCE BY ordering?
a: Duplicate current rows (multiple rows with __IS_CURRENT = TRUE for the same key) in an APPLY CHANGES SCD Type 2 target almost always trace to one of three sources. First, duplicate events in the streaming ingestion source: Auto Loader or the Kafka stream delivered the same event twice (e.g., exactly-once was not enforced, a retry produced a duplicate message with the same sequence value). APPLY CHANGES uses SEQUENCE BY to resolve ordering — if two events have the exact same SEQUENCE BY value and the same key, the engine cannot determine which is newer and may create two active rows. Fix: deduplicate the bronze source before APPLY CHANGES, either with a Lakeflow silver table using dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() on the streaming ingestion stream, or by adding a unique constraint check in the bronze Expectation layer. Second, the SEQUENCE BY column has NULL values: nulls sort unpredictably and APPLY CHANGES may treat two null-sequenced events for the same key as concurrent updates. Fix: add a Lakeflow Expectation @dlt.expect_or_drop to reject null SEQUENCE BY values before the APPLY CHANGES table. Third, the pipeline ran in full refresh mode after a schema change, which reset the APPLY CHANGES state and reprocessed events from the beginning — if the source retained old events with older sequence values, the merge may have produced duplicates during the catch-up. Fix: after a full refresh on SCD Type 2, run a post-validation query SELECT key, COUNT(*) FROM scd2_table WHERE __IS_CURRENT = TRUE GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to detect duplicates. Resolve by deduplicating with a MERGE that sets __IS_CURRENT = FALSE on older rows by __START_AT.
fj: I would run a DELETE to remove the duplicate rows and then continue.
fs: Duplicate __IS_CURRENT rows in SCD Type 2 APPLY CHANGES trace to duplicate events in the streaming ingestion source, null SEQUENCE BY values, or a full refresh reprocessing. Fix at source: dedup bronze with dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark, reject null SEQUENCE BY via Expectations, and validate after any full refresh.

followups.q: How does dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark differ from dropDuplicates in a Structured Streaming dedup context?
followups.a: dropDuplicates() in Structured Streaming maintains an unbounded state store — it remembers every key ever seen, which grows without bound and eventually causes OOM or excessive checkpoint size. dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() uses a watermark to bound the state: keys older than watermark delay are evicted from the dedup state. This is the correct pattern for streaming ingestion deduplication because it bounds memory usage at the cost of allowing duplicates that arrive beyond the watermark — acceptable when the source system guarantees late-arrival bounds.

q: How do you configure a Great Expectations Spark datasource to batch against a partitioned Delta table on Databricks, and what BatchRequest parameters control which partition is validated?
a: In the Great Expectations Fluent API (GX 0.17+) on Databricks you add a Spark datasource via context.sources.add_spark('dbx_spark'), then add a Delta table asset with add_delta_table_asset pointing at a Unity Catalog table or DBFS path. To partition the asset you call add_batch_definition_monthly_partitioned_on_column (or daily/yearly) with the partition column name. The resulting BatchRequest automatically includes a batch_identifiers dict with year/month keys, so the Checkpoint validates only the specified partition's Parquet files rather than scanning the full table. For manual partition control you pass options={'partitionFilters': [...]} or pre-filter the DataFrame in a RuntimeBatchRequest. The key operational benefit is that a 10 TB historical Delta table can be validated partition-by-partition in a daily job without triggering a full scan each run. Checkpoints should store the last-validated partition key in a Delta quality_log table so the job can resume from where it left off after a cluster failure.
fj: I'd just pass the full table path and let GX figure out the partition.
fs: Use BatchRequest partition parameters (add_batch_definition_*_partitioned_on_column) to scope Great Expectations scans to a single Delta table partition — prevents full-table scans on multi-TB tables and enables incremental daily validation.

code.label: GX Fluent API — partitioned Delta batch

followups.q: What happens if a Great Expectations Checkpoint runs on a Delta table while an OPTIMIZE job is compacting it?
followups.a: Delta Lake's snapshot isolation protects the Checkpoint: GX reads the Delta log snapshot at the moment the Spark job starts and sees only the Parquet files valid at that version. OPTIMIZE compacting files in parallel creates new Parquet files and a new Delta log commit, but those files are invisible to the already-started GX Spark job. The GX validation completes against a consistent snapshot. The only edge case is very long-running GX jobs that outlast the Delta log retention window — if VACUUM runs with a short retention before the GX job finishes reading old files, the job can fail with FileNotFoundException.

followups.q: How do you handle schema evolution in Great Expectations suites when the upstream Delta table adds a new column?
followups.a: Great Expectations Expectations that reference specific columns are unaffected by new columns being added. The suite still validates the declared columns. The risk is that new columns may be undeclared (no Expectations) and silently carry bad data. Best practice is to add expect_table_columns_to_match_ordered_list or expect_table_column_count_to_equal and set result_format='SUMMARY' so new columns surface as a validation warning. In CI, run suite autogeneration (profiler) on the new schema version and diff the generated suite against the existing one — the diff reveals new/removed columns before promotion.

q: Walk through how Great Expectations Checkpoints work on Databricks: what exactly runs when context.run_checkpoint() is called, and how do you persist ValidationResults to a Unity Catalog Volume?
a: When context.run_checkpoint('my_checkpoint') is called, GX resolves the Checkpoint config from the checkpoint_store (YAML or JSON file in the configured store, typically a UC Volume path), loads the referenced ExpectationSuites from the expectation_store, constructs BatchRequests, and calls the Validator for each (batch_request, suite) pair. Each Validator runs Spark aggregation queries against the SparkSession, collects success/failure statistics, and returns a ValidationResult object. The Checkpoint collects all ValidationResults into a CheckpointResult, writes each ValidationResult to the validations_store, and optionally triggers action_list items — which may include UpdateDataDocsAction (re-renders static HTML Data Docs), SlackNotificationAction, or a custom Python action. To back all stores with a Unity Catalog Volume set store_backend.class_name to TupleFilesystemStoreBackend and set base_directory to /Volumes/catalog/schema/vol/great_expectations/. The service principal running the Databricks job must have WRITE on that UC Volume path.
fj: I'd just call checkpoint.run() and it automatically saves everything.
fs: context.run_checkpoint() resolves store configs, runs Validators as Spark jobs, collects ValidationResults, writes them to the configured store (UC Volume in production), and fires action_list items including Data Docs update and alerting hooks.

code.label: Checkpoint config backed by UC Volume

followups.q: What does UpdateDataDocsAction do and when would you disable it in a Checkpoint?
followups.a: UpdateDataDocsAction regenerates the static HTML Data Docs site by merging the new ValidationResult into the existing HTML. On a UC Volume with many historical results this can be slow because GX rewrites the full index HTML on every run. In high-frequency jobs (hourly or per-micro-batch) disable UpdateDataDocsAction from the Checkpoint action_list and instead run a separate low-frequency job that calls context.build_data_docs() once per hour or per day. This keeps the Checkpoint fast while still publishing fresh Data Docs on a reasonable cadence.

followups.q: How do you pass runtime data (a streaming micro-batch DataFrame) to a Checkpoint rather than reading from a Delta table?
followups.a: Use a RuntimeBatchRequest instead of a standard BatchRequest. Pass batch_data=df (the micro-batch DataFrame) and set datasource_name to a Spark datasource, data_connector_name to runtime_data_connector, and data_asset_name to any string label. The Checkpoint receives the in-memory DataFrame directly without reading from storage, making it suitable for foreachBatch streaming validation. RuntimeBatchRequest bypasses the partition logic, so you also pass batch_identifiers to tag the result with a meaningful label (e.g. micro-batch timestamp).

q: How do you build and serve Great Expectations Data Docs on Databricks so that non-technical stakeholders can browse validation history without cluster access?
a: Data Docs are static HTML files generated by context.build_data_docs(). On Databricks the recommended production pattern in 2025+ is to store them on a Unity Catalog Volume (/Volumes/main/dq/data_docs/) and serve them via a Databricks-hosted static site or an external storage-backed CDN. The simplest approach: store_backend for the data_docs_sites section in great_expectations.yml points to a UC Volume path with a TupleFilesystemStoreBackend. After each Checkpoint run, UpdateDataDocsAction regenerates the HTML. To serve to stakeholders, either (a) expose the UC Volume via a Databricks Apps static file server (available in DBR 14+), (b) sync the Volume path to an S3 bucket behind CloudFront using an AWS Lambda triggered by S3 event or a cron Databricks job, or (c) use the Databricks Files API to periodically download and re-upload to an S3 static website bucket. For internal teams the Databricks notebook %sh approach — running python -m http.server inside a long-lived cluster — is not production-safe because it dies with the cluster. The UC Volume → S3 sync pattern decouples Data Docs availability from cluster lifecycle, which is the key SRE concern.
fj: I'd run python -m http.server on the cluster to serve Data Docs.
fs: Serve Great Expectations Data Docs by writing HTML to a Unity Catalog Volume then syncing to S3 static hosting or a Databricks Apps static server — decouples availability from cluster lifecycle so stakeholders can always browse validation history.

code.label: Data Docs site config in great_expectations.yml
lines: # After each checkpoint run, sync to S3:

followups.q: How do you scope Data Docs to show only the last 30 days of ValidationResults to avoid a bloated index?
followups.a: Great Expectations does not have a built-in TTL for Data Docs. The practical pattern is to periodically prune the validations_store: list all ValidationResult keys in the store, filter to those older than 30 days using the run_time metadata in the ValidationResult key, delete them from the TupleFilesystemStoreBackend, then call context.build_data_docs() to regenerate the site. This can be wrapped in a weekly Databricks job. For very high-volume pipelines, consider writing ValidationResult summaries to a Delta table instead of relying on Data Docs HTML for long-term history, and use Data Docs only as a live operational dashboard for the last 7 days.

q: Compare Great Expectations Checkpoints vs Lakeflow Expectations (DLT expect_or_drop / expect_or_fail) for enforcing data quality gates in a medallion pipeline — when does each win, and can you use both in the same pipeline?
a: Lakeflow Expectations (DLT native) are SQL predicate decorators applied to streaming table definitions — @dlt.expect_or_drop('non_null_id', 'id IS NOT NULL') runs per row at pipeline execution time with zero network overhead and writes violation counts to the DLT event log and UC data quality metrics. They are tight to the pipeline graph: declarative, version-controlled with the pipeline code, and first-class in the Databricks UI. Their limitation is scope — only SQL predicate logic, no statistical assertions, no cross-table checks, and no human-readable report outside the DLT UI. Great Expectations Checkpoints are invoked as a separate Python step (in a DLT Python notebook or a downstream job), run richer Expectations (distributional, regex, inter-column, custom Python), produce portable Data Docs HTML, and integrate with external alerting. Their limitation: they run outside the DLT execution graph, so they cannot use @expect_or_drop semantics; they are a separate Spark job after the table is written. Best practice: use both. Lakeflow Expectations for structural row-level guards (nulls, enum validity, range checks) inside the DLT pipeline — fast and native. Great Expectations Checkpoint in a downstream Databricks Job after the silver layer is written — for distribution drift, cross-column constraints, and stakeholder-visible Data Docs. The two layers are complementary, not competing.
fj: Great Expectations replaces Lakeflow Expectations since it is more powerful.
fs: Use Lakeflow Expectations for per-row structural guards inside DLT (zero overhead, native, first-class UI); use Great Expectations Checkpoints downstream for distributional assertions and Data Docs. They are complementary — neither replaces the other.

followups.q: How do you surface Great Expectations validation failures in the same Databricks Jobs run timeline as the DLT pipeline that wrote the data?
followups.a: Chain the DLT pipeline and the GX Checkpoint job in a Databricks Workflow. The Workflow DAG has two tasks: task_1 runs the DLT pipeline, task_2 runs a Databricks notebook or Python wheel job that calls context.run_checkpoint(). task_2 depends_on task_1. If the GX Checkpoint fails it raises an exception, which marks task_2 (and the overall Workflow run) as failed. The Jobs UI shows the failure inline with the DLT run, giving operators a single timeline. To avoid blocking the pipeline on GX, run the GX task as a parallel branch with a separate dependency (not blocking the main gold layer task).

followups.q: Can Lakeflow Expectations and Great Expectations share the same quarantine table?
followups.a: They can write to logically related tables but the mechanisms differ. Lakeflow @expect_or_drop writes dropped rows to the DLT quarantine table you define explicitly (a streaming table that reads from the _quarantine virtual dataset). GX cannot directly write to that DLT-managed table; instead the GX Checkpoint can write invalid rows identified by GX Expectations to a separate Delta table (via a custom Python ExpectationValidationResultsSubsetAction or a post-validation notebook step). A unified quarantine view can UNION both DLT-dropped rows and GX-identified rows for ops review, even though the write paths differ.

q: Describe a CI/CD pipeline that validates Great Expectations Expectation Suites on Databricks: how do you run GX in a GitHub Actions workflow against a Databricks cluster without shipping a full cluster for every PR?
a: The standard 2025 pattern runs Great Expectations in two tiers in CI. For PR-time unit tests (fast, no cluster): run pytest against sample Parquet files or pandas DataFrames locally in GitHub Actions using the Pandas datasource. The Expectations themselves are validated against a small representative dataset committed to the repo under tests/fixtures/. This catches broken Expectation syntax and obvious schema regressions in under a minute. For integration validation (post-merge or nightly): use the Databricks CLI or Databricks SDK to submit a one-off jobs run (databricks jobs run-now) that executes a parameterized Databricks notebook or Python wheel job on an existing cluster pool. The job runs context.run_checkpoint() against the real Delta table, writes ValidationResults to a UC Volume, and returns a non-zero exit code on failure. GitHub Actions polls the job run status via jobs.get_run() until terminal state, then fails the workflow if success=false. Critically, the cluster is a pre-warmed job cluster pool so startup overhead is seconds not minutes, and the cluster terminates after the job — no long-running cluster cost. Suite files live in the repo under great_expectations/expectations/ and are synced to the UC Volume at job start via the Databricks Files API or a UC Volume mount. This ensures Suite version is pinned to the git commit being tested.
fj: I'd just run GX locally and manually check results before merging.
fs: Two-tier GX CI: fast Pandas-backed pytest in GitHub Actions for PR-time syntax checks; Databricks Jobs run-now for post-merge Spark integration validation against real Delta tables on a cluster pool. Suite files live in git and sync to UC Volume at job start.

followups.q: How do you version-control Great Expectations Expectation Suites alongside the pipeline code that writes the Delta table?
followups.a: Store the great_expectations/ directory (including expectations/, checkpoints/, and great_expectations.yml) in the same git repository as the pipeline code — not in a separate repo. Use a monorepo layout where pipelines/silver/orders/ and great_expectations/expectations/silver_orders_suite.json are siblings. On every merge to main, a CI step syncs the expectations/ directory to the UC Volume via databricks fs cp or the Files API. This pins the Suite version to the same commit as the pipeline logic. For backward compatibility, name Suites with a version suffix (silver_orders_suite_v2.json) and update Checkpoint configs to reference the new suite name, keeping the old suite available for rollback.

followups.q: How do you test that a new Expectation does not create false positives on historical data before adding it to the suite?
followups.a: Use the GX profiler or run the candidate Expectation in observe mode (expect_column_values_to_not_be_null with result_format='COMPLETE') against a sample of historical data. Run the Expectation without adding it to the suite, inspect the observed unexpected_count and unexpected_percent. If unexpected_percent is above an acceptable threshold (e.g. > 0.1%) on clean historical data, the Expectation is too strict or the historical data has legitimate edge cases — adjust the threshold or add an exception filter. Only after the false-positive rate is acceptable on sampled history do you add the Expectation to the suite and commit.

q: How do you write a custom Great Expectations Expectation in Python on Databricks that validates a business rule spanning multiple columns, and how do you register it so Checkpoints can use it?
a: Great Expectations supports custom Expectations via the ColumnPairMapExpectation, MulticolumnMapExpectation, or BatchExpectation base classes. For a multi-column rule — e.g. 'if status is SHIPPED then shipped_at must not be null' — subclass ColumnPairMapExpectation (or MulticolumnMapExpectation for 3+ columns). Override map_fn_batch_kwargs to declare which columns are passed to the validation kernel, and implement _sqlalchemy_dialect_func (for SQL-backed engines) or _spark_df_validate (for Spark). In the Spark path _spark_df_validate receives the full DataFrame; return a column expression (F.when(...).otherwise(...)) that evaluates to True for valid rows. Decorate the class with @great_expectations.core.expectation_configuration.expectation_parameters to declare parameters. Register the Expectation by placing the Python file in the plugins/custom_expectations/ directory under the GX project root and adding it to great_expectations.yml under plugins_directory. On Databricks, install the custom Expectations file on the cluster via cluster init script or by placing it in a UC Volume path that the job cluster mounts. The custom Expectation then appears in the Expectation Gallery and can be called by name in Suites and Checkpoints.
fj: I'd write a Python assert inside the Spark job and raise an exception — custom GX Expectations are too complex.
fs: Subclass ColumnPairMapExpectation or MulticolumnMapExpectation to encode multi-column business rules; implement the Spark path via _spark_df_validate returning a Column expression; register via plugins_directory in great_expectations.yml and deploy to the cluster via init script or UC Volume.

followups.q: How do you unit-test a custom Great Expectations Expectation without a running Spark cluster?
followups.a: Use the GX built-in test harness: define examples in the Expectation class under the examples class attribute, where each example provides input data (as a list of dicts), expected_results (success True/False), and a title. Running pytest against the Expectation class triggers GX to execute the examples with a local Pandas engine if the Expectation supports it. For Spark-only Expectations, use a PySpark local mode session in pytest fixtures — spark = SparkSession.builder.master('local[1]').getOrCreate() — to run the Spark path without a Databricks cluster. This lets CI validate custom Expectation logic in GitHub Actions on a standard runner without cluster cost.

followups.q: What is the difference between a ColumnMapExpectation and a BatchExpectation, and when would you use a BatchExpectation for a Great Expectations data quality check on Databricks?
followups.a: ColumnMapExpectation operates row-by-row on a single column and reports unexpected_count/unexpected_percent — suitable for null checks, regex, range bounds. BatchExpectation operates on the entire batch (DataFrame or table) and returns a single aggregate result — suitable for table-level assertions like total row count, column count, referential checks against another table (via a subquery), or cross-batch volume anomaly checks. On Databricks, a BatchExpectation that checks row count trend by querying a Delta quality_log table is a common pattern: expect_table_row_count_to_be_between with dynamic min/max computed from a trailing 7-day average serves as a volume anomaly detector without a separate ML model.

q: What is Deequ and how does it differ architecturally from Great Expectations on Databricks?
a: Deequ (open-sourced by Amazon, Python surface via PyDeequ) is a data-quality library built natively on top of Apache Spark's DataFrame API. Every Deequ Check compiles into a single Spark computation graph — Analyzers aggregate metrics in one physical scan, then Constraints assert conditions on those metrics. Great Expectations, by contrast, runs per-expectation transformations and can trigger multiple passes over the data. On Databricks this means Deequ scales with the cluster naturally, incurs minimal extra scans, and stores versioned metrics in a MetricsRepository (backed by S3/DBFS/Delta) for trend analysis. GE brings a richer ecosystem of built-in expectations and a broader integration catalog but typically requires more careful optimization on large tables.
fj: Deequ is just an older library that does the same thing as Great Expectations.
fs: Deequ compiles all Checks into one Spark scan via Analyzers; GE runs per-expectation transforms. Deequ is natively Spark-native with a built-in MetricsRepository; GE has a broader ecosystem but needs extra optimization on large tables.
