q: In a Spark stream-stream join, what state does Spark maintain on each side and why does it grow unbounded if you omit watermarks?
fs: One watermark on one stream is not enough. Spark requires watermarks on BOTH input streams plus an event-time range join condition to derive a cleanup bound. A single watermark gives Spark no way to know when rows on the other (un-watermarked) side are safe to drop.
a: When two streaming DataFrames are joined, Spark cannot know at processing time whether a row from stream A will eventually match a row from stream B that hasn't arrived yet. So it must buffer all past rows from BOTH streams in state storage (the RocksDB or HDFS state store) and attempt to match each new incoming row against every buffered row on the other side. Without watermarks and event-time range constraints there is no signal telling Spark that a buffered row can never match a future row — so every row is kept forever. State grows as O(N * M) where N and M are the row counts of each stream since query start, eventually exhausting memory or disk. To bound state you must: (1) declare withWatermark on both streams' event-time columns; (2) add a time-range join condition (e.g. clickTime BETWEEN impressionTime AND impressionTime + INTERVAL 1 HOUR). Together these let Spark calculate a cleanup bound: once the global watermark has advanced past the latest possible match time for a buffered row, that row is evicted from state. Without BOTH of these, state is not cleaned up even for inner joins.

q: When two input streams each have a watermark in a stream-stream join, how does Spark compute the single global watermark and what happens when one stream falls behind?
fs: Watermarks are not independent in a join. Spark merges them into a single global watermark using the min (by default). A slow stream pins the global watermark for ALL stateful operations in the query, blocking state cleanup and outer-join NULL emission across the board.
a: Spark individually tracks the maximum observed event time for each input stream and applies each stream's declared delay to get a per-stream watermark. It then computes a single global watermark using the MINIMUM of the two per-stream watermarks by default. This global watermark is what drives state eviction and, for outer joins, controls when NULL rows are emitted. The min policy is deliberately conservative: if stream B has stalled (upstream outage, partition lag), its watermark stops advancing. Even if stream A's events are hours ahead, the global watermark is pinned at stream B's last-seen event minus B's delay — no state is cleaned up and no outer NULLs are emitted until B recovers. The trade-off is safety over latency: the min policy guarantees you never drop data from the slow stream, but your end-to-end latency is bounded by the slowest producer. You can override this by setting spark.sql.streaming.multipleWatermarkPolicy to max — the global watermark then tracks the fastest stream and state is cleaned up aggressively, but any rows from the slow stream that arrive after the watermark has advanced are silently dropped. In production, always monitor both streams' watermark lag via StreamingQueryProgress.eventTime; a widening gap between the two watermarks is an early warning that your state store is growing and latency is climbing.

q: Auto Loader schema evolution: what are the four cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode values and which do you use in production?
fs: schemaLocation is required to persist the inferred schema across restarts. Without it, Auto Loader re-infers from scratch every run, which is slow and inconsistent. The default addNewColumns mode fails (and auto-restarts) on new columns — that's intentional, but you must configure a Job retry policy or the stream stays down.
a: Auto Loader infers schema by sampling the first 50 GB or 1,000 files and persists it to cloudFiles.schemaLocation (a _schemas directory at that path). When the source schema changes, the behavior is controlled by cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode: addNewColumns (the default when no explicit schema is provided) restarts the stream with an UnknownFieldException when a new column is detected, incorporating the wider schema on restart — pair this with a Jobs retry to self-heal; rescue captures new columns silently in _rescued_data without ever failing the stream — use this when you can tolerate deferred handling of new fields; failOnNewColumns stops the stream permanently and requires a human to update the schema or remove the file — use this when an unexpected column is a pipeline contract violation; none ignores all new columns entirely, dropping them. The production default for unattended pipelines is addNewColumns with auto-restart, because it surfaces new columns immediately rather than silently losing them. If you can't afford a restart gap, rescue is the safe fallback. Never use none when data loss is unacceptable.

q: COPY INTO idempotency: how does it track which files have already been loaded, and when would you override it?
fs: Skipping is correct for the happy path, but when you need to reload corrected files you must either use COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'true') with a narrow PATTERN or FILES list, or delete the specific rows from the target and re-run. Running FORCE without a filter reloads everything and duplicates all previously ingested data.
a: COPY INTO maintains internal metadata about every file it has successfully loaded into the target Delta table. On each subsequent run it skips any file already recorded, even if the file has been modified since it was loaded — the tracking is by file path, not by content hash. This makes it safe to put COPY INTO in a scheduled job without deduplication logic. The override is the FORCE option (COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'true')), which disables idempotency entirely and reloads every matching file regardless of prior loads. Use FORCE when you have corrected upstream files and need to reload them, or during a targeted historical backfill on a specific path subset. The FILES = () clause lets you load only named files on demand — a cleaner alternative to FORCE when you know exactly which files need reprocessing. The common mistake is running COPY INTO with FORCE on a broad path to fix one bad file and inadvertently duplicating all other data — always combine FORCE with a narrow PATTERN or FILES filter.

code.label: COPY INTO — idempotent batch load (thousands of files)
Auto Loader — streaming ingest (millions of files)

q: COPY INTO vs Auto Loader: when do you use each for ingesting files from cloud storage into Delta?
fs: COPY INTO is correct up to thousands of files lifetime but gets expensive at scale because it must scan the full source path. Auto Loader is the production choice for millions of files — it uses cloud event notifications or incremental listing to stay O(new files), not O(total files).
a: The decision is volume-driven. COPY INTO is a SQL command that tracks which files have already been loaded and skips them on re-runs, making it safe to schedule repeatedly. It works in Databricks SQL, notebooks, and Jobs, and is the right choice when you expect files in the order of thousands over the table's lifetime — ad-hoc loads, one-time migrations, or pipelines where a small batch arrives daily. Auto Loader (the cloudFiles Structured Streaming source) is designed for millions of files or more: it uses cloud notification services or directory listing to detect new arrivals incrementally, requires fewer total listing API calls than COPY INTO at scale, and can split a large backlog across many micro-batches. Use Auto Loader for production streaming ingest and for any source with frequent, high-volume file drops. Auto Loader also offers richer schema inference and evolution primitives. Both tools support idempotent re-runs, but Auto Loader's Structured Streaming checkpoint is the more durable mechanism for long-running pipelines. The junior mistake is defaulting to COPY INTO in a loop when file counts will grow into the millions — listing cost becomes prohibitive.

q: What is the _rescued_data column in Auto Loader, exactly what lands there, and how do you parse it downstream?
fs: _rescued_data is only empty when the schema is stable. In rescue mode it fills silently on schema drift — no failure, no alert. If you don't monitor it and parse rescued fields into silver, you're losing data without knowing it. Wire a quality check on COUNT(*) WHERE _rescued_data IS NOT NULL as a pipeline alert.
a: _rescued_data is a STRING column Auto Loader automatically appends to every bronze table it writes. A row's unmatched data lands there as a JSON blob in three situations: the source has a column that is not in the current inferred schema (new field); the column exists in the schema but the value cannot be cast to the declared type; or the column name differs only in casing from the schema's declared name (case mismatch). The blob always includes the rescued column values keyed by their original source names, plus a special _filepath key containing the source file path for traceability. In rescue evolution mode, this column is the only signal of schema drift — no stream restart, no alert. To act on rescued data downstream you parse it with from_json and then either promote specific keys into first-class columns in silver, route rows with non-null _rescued_data to a quarantine table, or alert when the rescue rate rises above a threshold. The mistake is writing downstream queries that ignore _rescued_data — you silently lose all drifted fields.

code.label: AUTO CDC INTO — SCD Type 1 (latest state wins)
AUTO CDC INTO — SCD Type 2 (full history)

q: When does AUTO CDC INTO (formerly APPLY CHANGES INTO) in DLT simplify CDC ingestion compared to writing a manual MERGE?
fs: foreachBatch MERGE works but forces you to handle ordering and deduplication yourself. AUTO CDC INTO in a DLT pipeline manages sequence-based conflict resolution declaratively. It handles out-of-order events, SCD Type 1 and 2, and late deletes without custom code — and it integrates with the pipeline event log for observability.
a: A manual MERGE for CDC requires you to handle: reading the source stream, deduplicating within a micro-batch, ordering events by a sequence column to resolve late/out-of-order records, writing an idempotent MERGE keyed on the business key, and separately implementing SCD Type 2 if you need history. AUTO CDC INTO (the Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines CDC API, previously APPLY CHANGES INTO) collapses all of that into a single declarative statement. You declare the target table, the source stream, the KEYS columns, and a SEQUENCE BY column (a monotonically increasing value like a CDC offset or updated_at timestamp); the pipeline engine handles deduplication, out-of-order arrival, and applies each change as the correct operation. SEQUENCE BY is the key concept: if a DELETE at sequence 10 arrives before an UPDATE at sequence 8, the engine correctly resolves the final state as deleted, because it orders by the sequence column, not by arrival time. SCD Type 2 requires adding STORED AS SCD TYPE 2. Use AUTO CDC INTO any time your source is a change log (Debezium, Kafka CDC, DMS) and you want the pipeline to own correctness; use a manual MERGE when you need to run outside a Lakeflow pipeline context or need fine-grained control the API does not expose.

q: How do you query the DLT event log to track data quality expectation violation trends across pipeline runs?
fs: The UI tab shows the latest run only. For trending, degradation detection, and SLA alerting you need to query the event log directly. Wire a Databricks SQL dashboard or alert against the event_log expectation metrics — that's the production monitoring pattern.
a: Every Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline writes a hidden Delta event log table. For pipelines running in Unity Catalog, you access it with the event_log() table-valued function; for legacy pipelines you query the hidden table directly via a view you create at pipeline setup. The event log has columns: id, timestamp, event_type, details (JSON), origin (JSON containing pipeline_id, update_id, flow_name), and level. Data quality metrics live inside flow_progress events at details:flow_progress:data_quality:expectations — an array of structs with name, dataset, passed_records, and failed_records. To build a trend, explode that array, group by update_id + expectation name, and join origin.timestamp to get a time series. Alert when failed_records / (passed_records + failed_records) exceeds your SLA threshold. The mistake is relying only on the pipeline UI's Data Quality tab — it shows the last run, not history. Only event log queries give you week-over-week trends to catch slow degradation.

q: DLT pipeline expectations: what is the difference between EXPECT, EXPECT OR DROP, and EXPECT OR FAIL, and how do you choose?
fs: Using EXPECT OR FAIL at bronze halts ingestion on the first bad source record — that's too strict. A tiered approach matches enforcement to impact: warn at bronze (never block), drop at silver (clean the curated layer), fail at gold (protect aggregates and reports). A single bad source row should not take down your whole pipeline.
a: Databricks Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines (formerly DLT) offers three violation modes for row-level quality rules. EXPECT (warn): failing rows are written to the target table and the violation is counted in the event log — the pipeline continues. Use this at bronze where you never want ingestion to halt; you're surfacing quality signals without blocking data flow. EXPECT OR DROP: failing rows are silently removed before the data is written to the target. Use this at silver to produce a clean, curated dataset — downstream consumers get only valid rows, and the dropped count is visible in the event log for monitoring. EXPECT OR FAIL: any failing row halts the entire pipeline update with an error. Use this at gold or for aggregates where a bad row would corrupt a metric, a report, or a downstream SLA — the pipeline should stop rather than silently produce wrong answers. The Python decorators are @dp.expect, @dp.expect_or_drop, @dp.expect_or_fail; in SQL, add CONSTRAINT name EXPECT (predicate) [ON VIOLATION DROP ROW | FAIL UPDATE]. The expect_all / expect_all_or_drop / expect_all_or_fail variants accept a dict of named constraints so you can define a reusable quality contract in one place.

q: DLT expectations vs Structured Streaming watermarks: which handles late-arriving data, and which enforces row quality — and why do senior engineers confuse them?
fs: That expectation drops rows based on ingestion-time comparison, not event-time windowing — it breaks replay and backfill. Late data is a temporal state problem: use withWatermark on your streaming aggregation to bound state and drop events outside your lateness window. Expectations are for row-quality rules that are replay-safe and time-agnostic.
a: They solve different problems. Structured Streaming watermarks are a temporal state-management mechanism: withWatermark tells the engine how long to retain aggregation state for a given event-time window, and drops events that arrive after the watermark threshold has passed. Watermarks are about WHEN data arrived relative to event time — they don't inspect the data's content. DLT expectations are row-level quality predicates: they evaluate whether a column value satisfies a business rule (amount > 0, id IS NOT NULL) at the moment the row is processed. Expectations have no concept of time or arrival order. The confusion arises because both can discard rows — a watermark drops late events, an EXPECT OR DROP removes rows failing a quality rule — but for entirely different reasons. In a DLT pipeline, if you need to handle late-arriving CDC events you use AUTO CDC INTO with SEQUENCE BY, which resolves out-of-order changes by sequence number, not by arrival time (and has nothing to do with expectations). If you need temporal window aggregations within a DLT pipeline on a streaming table, you add withWatermark to the DataFrame before writing — expectations stack on top of that to validate the aggregated result. The senior trap is trying to use an expectation predicate to 'filter out late data' based on a timestamp column threshold — that's watermarking, not data quality.

Decision tree: if the source data is malformed (e.g., corrupt upstream feed), fix upstream and trigger a full-refresh pipeline run to reprocess the window; if the expectation rule is too strict (e.g., a new merchant type not yet in your allowlist), update the expectation logic and re-run. Reprocessing is safe in DLT because Delta's transaction log makes reruns idempotent. Never adjust EXPECT to EXPECT_OR_DROP without understanding the business impact — dropping rows silently in silver means gold aggregates are silently wrong.

q: Your Databricks job runs OPTIMIZE on a 10TB Delta table nightly, but it's taking 4+ hours and blocking downstream reads. What are your options?
fs: Throwing compute at a monolithic nightly OPTIMIZE shortens the window but doesn't fix the design. The correct interventions are partitioned OPTIMIZE sharding (narrow locks), auto-compaction (spread the work across writes), or Liquid Clustering (incremental, no manual tune) — not just more workers.
a: OPTIMIZE rewrites files and holds a commit lock during the transaction — on a 10TB unpartitioned table this is a multi-hour window that blocks other writers (readers are protected by snapshot isolation and can still read the previous version). Three options: (1) Shard OPTIMIZE by partition: run OPTIMIZE on one partition at a time in parallel jobs so the lock is narrow and short per-shard; (2) Enable autoOptimize.autoCompact on the table so Databricks spreads small-file compaction continuously across writes instead of doing it all in one nightly job — works best if write frequency is high; (3) Migrate to Liquid Clustering. ALTER TABLE <name> CLUSTER BY (<cols>) — requires DBR 13.3 LTS or above — and then OPTIMIZE runs incrementally, only rewriting files that contain data needing re-clustering. Liquid Clustering's key advantage: no manual ZORDER tuning, no partition scheme to maintain, and OPTIMIZE is safe to run frequently because it only rewrites what needs it. The catch: CLUSTER BY applies to new data immediately; existing files need OPTIMIZE FULL (DBR 16.0+) to be rewritten with the new clustering. For most 10TB+ production tables at Databricks shops, Liquid Clustering is now the recommended path.
followups: What is the difference between OPTIMIZE and OPTIMIZE FULL for Liquid Clustered tables?

lines: # Query the LHM profile metrics table

q: A revenue metric has been wrong in your gold layer for 3 weeks. Root cause: a currency conversion column was silently NULL because EXPECT didn't FAIL. What architecture changes prevent this class of issue?
fs: A NOT NULL constraint raises an error at write time but doesn't help if the column IS being written (with NULLs). The correct fix is EXPECT OR FAIL to block the pipeline if the invariant is violated — plus Lakehouse Monitoring to catch statistical drift (e.g., 10% null rate) before it compounds for weeks.
a: EXPECT in DLT logs violations and keeps moving — it is a monitoring signal, not a gate. On a metric column that feeds financial reporting, that is the wrong default. Three architectural changes: (1) Replace EXPECT with EXPECT OR FAIL on any column that is load-bearing for aggregations. EXPECT OR FAIL stops the pipeline and rolls back the update atomically — a failed run is noisy but a silent wrong number is worse; (2) Add Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring on the gold table. Lakehouse Monitoring tracks percent_null per column across time windows and can alert via Databricks SQL alerts when null fraction for fx_rate exceeds a threshold; (3) Write unit tests for your transformation notebooks using pytest or Databricks' test utilities — assert that the output of your currency conversion logic has zero NULLs on a representative fixture. The root-cause lesson: the issue propagated for 3 weeks because there was no fail-fast at the point of transformation, and no downstream anomaly detection. Defense-in-depth: EXPECT OR FAIL at pipeline time + Lakehouse Monitoring alert + transformation unit tests.

fj: I'd run a daily OPTIMIZE job to compact the small files.

code.label: Enable auto-compaction on the Delta table
lines: -- numFiles column shows files per version

q: A Structured Streaming job writes to a Delta table and your monitoring shows the file count growing unboundedly. What's happening and how do you fix it?
fs: A scheduled OPTIMIZE is a good fallback, but it adds operational overhead and a brief table lock. The idiomatic Databricks fix is enabling autoOptimize.optimizeWrite and autoOptimize.autoCompact as table properties — they compact continuously at write time without a separate job.
a: Each micro-batch appends a new set of Parquet files to the Delta table. With a 30-second trigger and small batch sizes, you accumulate 2,880+ files per day on a single table — every read now must open and scan hundreds of tiny files, causing metadata overhead and slow query times. There are three production fixes: (1) Set autoOptimize.optimizeWrite=true as a table property — Databricks dynamically coalesces files during the write itself, producing fewer larger files without extra overhead; (2) Set autoOptimize.autoCompact=true — Databricks runs a lightweight OPTIMIZE after each write, further merging small files in the background; (3) Schedule an explicit OPTIMIZE + ZORDER on a maintenance job (nightly, off-peak) for heavier compaction and skipping. The trade-off: options 1 and 2 add slight per-batch write latency; scheduled OPTIMIZE acquires a short table lock that can delay concurrent reads. On tables with Liquid Clustering, OPTIMIZE automatically clusters data incrementally — no ZORDER needed. Use DESCRIBE HISTORY to see file counts per version and verify improvement.
followups: What is the difference between optimizeWrite and autoCompact in terms of when compaction happens?
How does Liquid Clustering change the OPTIMIZE behavior for streaming tables?

code.label: foreachBatch: re-reads merchant snapshot each batch
lines: # Re-reads merchants at batch time, not job start

q: Your streaming pipeline joins incoming transactions against a merchants dimension table updated daily. About 2% of records are missing merchant enrichment. Walk through why this happens and how you fix it.
fs: A NULL default masks a real data gap. The right fix is eliminating the race: either gate the stream start on the merchant load completing, or use a foreachBatch MERGE so the driver re-reads the merchants snapshot after each batch — not a null coalesce.
a: In a standard stream-static join, Databricks refreshes the static Delta table snapshot at each micro-batch — so in theory, today's merchants are available. The 2% miss pattern is a timing issue: the daily merchant load and the streaming micro-batch can race. If the micro-batch runs before the merchant pipeline finishes its MERGE/OVERWRITE, those new merchants aren't visible yet. Transactions for brand-new merchants processed in that window get a NULL enrichment and the join drops them. Fix strategies ranked by reliability: (1) Schedule the streaming job to start after the merchant pipeline completes (simplest, fragile); (2) Use a MERGE in foreachBatch against the Delta merchants table — the Spark driver re-reads the latest snapshot at each batch start, and your write can use WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT to fill the gap; (3) Keep a 'late enrichment' path — write unenriched rows to a quarantine table, then replay them once the merchant record is present. The production trap is assuming the join is purely a static read — the race between pipelines is the real bug.
followups: What does 'left anti join' against a quarantine table give you for replaying unenriched records?
How do you make foreachBatch writes idempotent if the batch is reprocessed?

fj: I'd use dropDuplicates with withWatermark to handle the duplicates.

code.label: Unbounded state: will OOM on long-running jobs
Bounded state: evicts after watermark advances

q: You're building a fraud-detection streaming pipeline that processes 500K+ transactions/day. Duplicate events arrive within a 5-minute window. How do you deduplicate without dropping valid retries?
fs: dropDuplicates() without watermark-aware eviction keeps unbounded state and will OOM. Use dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() (Spark 3.5+) so state expires once the watermark advances — state is bounded to your replay window, not to job lifetime.
a: The naive approach — dropDuplicates() paired with withWatermark() — keeps unbounded state: Spark must retain every seen key indefinitely because it can't know when the watermark column isn't in the dedup key, so the state store grows without bound and eventually OOMs on a 500K+/day pipeline. The correct tool since Spark 3.5.0 is dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark(). It evicts state for keys once the event-time watermark advances past them, bounding state to the watermark window. Pair it with a watermark of at least your replay window (5 minutes here, with headroom): .withWatermark('eventTime', '10 minutes').dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark(['txn_id']). For the sink side, use a Delta MERGE ON txn_id so any duplicate that slips through the watermark window is idempotent at write time — a two-layer defense. The key interview tell: unbounded state is the production kill, not logical duplicates.
followups: What's the trade-off of a wider watermark window on deduplication guarantees?
How does foreachBatch with MERGE give you idempotent exactly-once semantics at the sink?

code.label: What they ran — bypassing the safety check
lines: RETAIN 0 HOURS;  -- deletes all old Parquet files

code.label: Production governance — prevent this on audit tables
lines: -- require it as a guard in your runbook.

q: You need rows from a Delta table as they existed 7 days ago for a regulatory audit. But a colleague ran VACUUM with the retention set to 0 hours yesterday. What are your options?
fs: Time travel reads the old data files — but VACUUM with 0-hour retention deleted those files. The transaction log version entries exist but the underlying Parquet is gone. The correct response is: data is unrecoverable via time travel; escalate to backups and upstream source replay, then fix the retention policy.
a: Time travel is gone — VACUUM with a 0-hour retention deletes all data files not referenced by the current table version. Delta's transaction log entries (in _delta_log/) still exist but they point to Parquet files that have been deleted. Your options: (1) Restore from a backup snapshot if your organization takes regular Delta table snapshots or uses Databricks' managed backup; (2) Check Unity Catalog DESCRIBE HISTORY — if any older checkpoint or log file references external storage you control, data may still be there; (3) Escalate to your data engineering team to pull from the upstream source (Kafka replay, S3 raw zone, change-data-capture log) and reprocess the 7-day window. For prevention: never set retentionDuration below 7 days (the default). Running VACUUM with 0-hour retention requires explicitly disabling the safety check (SET spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled = false) — this flag exists precisely to block accidental data loss. Production governance: set delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration = '30 days' on audit-critical tables, and require a ticket/approval before any VACUUM below 7 days. Databricks strongly recommends a minimum of 7 days retention.
followups: What is the difference between delta.logRetentionDuration and delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration?

fj: Delta doesn't support concurrent writes, so I need to serialize everything.

code.label: Overlap: both jobs scan all files
lines: -- Job A and Job B both run:
MERGE INTO events t USING src s

code.label: Partition-isolated: disjoint file sets
lines: MERGE INTO events t USING src_us s

q: Two jobs are MERGEing the same Delta table simultaneously. One fails with ConcurrentAppendException. What caused it and how do you prevent it?
fs: Delta handles concurrent writes via optimistic concurrency — conflicts only occur when writers scan overlapping files. Partition-isolate your MERGEs (include the partition col in ON) so file sets are disjoint and commits never conflict.
a: Delta uses optimistic concurrency: each writer reads the current snapshot, stages new Parquet files, then races to commit the next version number to the transaction log. At commit time Delta checks whether any files it read have been added or modified by a concurrent writer. When two MERGE jobs scan overlapping sets of files — even on logically different rows — the second commit sees that the files it read have already changed and throws ConcurrentAppendException. The fix is partition isolation: include the partition column in the MERGE ON clause so each writer's file scan is constrained to disjoint partitions and the commit validator sees no overlap. Where true overlap is unavoidable (both jobs can write the same rows), serialize the writers or implement retry-with-backoff since idempotent MERGEs are safe to replay. On recent runtimes, enabling deletion vectors gives row-level conflict detection, reducing false conflicts between writers touching different rows in the same file. The wrong answer is 'Delta can't handle concurrent writes' — it can, as long as file sets are disjoint.

fj: I'd omit the watermark so I never drop late events.
q: Your nightly report ran at midnight. At 2am, events timestamped for yesterday arrive and the report is wrong. How do you fix it without a full recompute?
fs: No watermark = unbounded state = eventual OOM. Bound it to your real lateness SLA; correct gold via MERGE on grain, not full overwrite.
a: There are two problems: unbounded streaming state and a non-idempotent gold table. On the stream, add withWatermark on the event-time column bounded to your real lateness SLA (e.g. 2 hours) so Spark drops state older than the watermark and stops accumulating memory indefinitely. For the gold correction, write an idempotent MERGE keyed on the output grain so a late-arriving batch corrects only the affected rows rather than duplicating them. For corrections that arrive beyond the watermark window, use replaceWhere to atomically reprocess just the affected partition from bronze without touching the rest of the table. The wrong instinct is omitting withWatermark, which means state grows forever and the job eventually OOMs; equally wrong is overwriting the full gold table on each late batch.

fj: I'd overwrite the full table to be safe.
q: Yesterday's load had a bug. How do you reprocess only that one day on a multi-TB Delta table — without rebuilding the whole table or breaking in-flight reads?
fs: Full overwrite rewrites TBs for a one-day fix. replaceWhere is one atomic commit that touches only the matching rows — reads never see an inconsistent state.
a: Use replaceWhere: write the corrected data with mode('overwrite') and .option('replaceWhere', "event_date='2024-01-14'") and Delta atomically replaces only the rows matching that predicate in a single commit, leaving all other data untouched. Reads that started before the commit see the old snapshot; reads after see the corrected one — no window of inconsistency. The predicate must match your actual data (Delta validates and fails the write if rows fall outside it by default). Reprocess from bronze, not the source system, so the pipeline is reproducible. The wrong answers are overwriting the whole table (unnecessary full rewrite of TBs) and DELETE + INSERT (two separate commits = a window where the partition is empty).

fj: I'd UPDATE the customer's address in place so the table stays current.

code.label: Two-branch MERGE to close old + insert new

q: A customer's address changes. The business needs to know the address as it was at the time of each past order. How do you model and maintain this in Delta Lake?
fs: UPDATE in place is SCD Type 1 — it destroys history. SCD Type 2 inserts a new version and closes the old one, so every past order still joins to the address that was true then.
a: This is SCD Type 2. The table carries a surrogate key (auto-incremented or UUID), a natural business key (customer_id), the tracked attributes, an effective_from timestamp, an effective_to timestamp (NULL for the current row), and an is_current boolean. On a change you run a two-branch MERGE: the WHEN MATCHED branch closes the old current row by setting effective_to and is_current = false; the WHEN NOT MATCHED branch inserts the new row as current. The merge_key trick is to union the source with itself — one copy with the real key (to match and close the old row), one copy with a NULL key (to force a NOT MATCHED insert for the new version). In Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines you use APPLY CHANGES INTO ... STORED AS SCD TYPE 2, which handles the row management and out-of-order events via SEQUENCE BY. The wrong answer is a plain UPDATE in place (SCD Type 1 — overwrites history).
